title: How a Bipolar Diagnosis Became the Foundation for a Million-Dollar PR Empire
guestBio: Gabrielle Scout Maio is the founder and CEO of Scouts Agency and author of The Emotional Entrepreneur. Diagnosed with bipolar disorder at 14, she transformed her mental health journey into entrepreneurial fuel, building a PR empire that has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes and amplified hundreds of female voices.
excerpt: What if the thing you think disqualifies you from success is actually your greatest asset? Gabrielle Scout Maio proves that emotional intelligence trumps hustle every time, building a PR empire while redefining what powerful leadership looks like.
metaDescription: From bipolar diagnosis to PR empire: Gabrielle Scout Maio's journey proves emotional intelligence beats hustle in building authentic success.
keyTakeaways: Mental health challenges can become entrepreneurial superpowers when properly channeled and understood.
Emotional intelligence is more valuable than traditional hustle culture in building sustainable business success.
Being early to innovation is difficult but creates lasting competitive advantages in business.
Motherhood can actually accelerate business performance by forcing efficiency and clarity of priorities.
Authentic leadership that embraces vulnerability creates stronger teams and better business outcomes.
Women leaders naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them through their leadership style.
Your pain has purpose and your story has power when channeled into meaningful work.

pullQuotes.text: Emotional intelligence — not hustle — is the real secret to success
Your pain has purpose. Your story has power

timestamps.label: From Mental Health Diagnosis to Entrepreneurial Vision
Building Scouts Agency and the Podcast Revolution

content:
What if everything you've been told about your limitations is actually a lie? What if the diagnosis that was supposed to define your struggles became the foundation for your greatest success? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with Gabrielle Scout Maio to explore one of the most compelling entrepreneurial journeys you'll ever hear.

At 14, Gabrielle received a bipolar diagnosis that came with a crushing prediction: she would struggle to hold a job. Instead of accepting this limitation, she discovered something revolutionary. Entrepreneurship wasn't just a career path for her—it was her superpower. Seven years after launching Scouts Agency in 2019, she's built a PR empire that has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes and amplified hundreds of female voices across the globe.

Transforming Pain into Purpose

Gabrielle's story challenges everything we think we know about success and mental health. Rather than hiding from her diagnosis or letting it define her limitations, she learned to harness the very traits that made her different. The emotional depth that comes with bipolar disorder became her greatest business asset, allowing her to connect with clients on a level that traditional PR agencies simply couldn't match.

This transformation didn't happen overnight. It required Gabrielle to completely reframe her relationship with her mental health, viewing it not as a weakness to overcome but as a strength to leverage. She discovered that the emotional intelligence required to navigate bipolar disorder translated directly into the kind of authentic leadership that builds lasting businesses.

The Birth of a PR Revolution

When Gabrielle launched Scouts Agency, she wasn't just starting another PR firm—she was pioneering an entirely new approach to public relations. She recognized early that podcast tours would become the future of PR, long before the industry caught on. Being early to this innovation wasn't easy. It required constant education of potential clients and the courage to stick with a vision when others couldn't see it yet.

Within just three months of launching, Gabrielle was able to quit her traditional job. But the real magic happened in how she built the business differently. Instead of following the typical agency playbook of grinding and hustling, she created a model based on genuine relationships, emotional intelligence, and authentic storytelling. This approach didn't just work—it revolutionized how her clients showed up in the world.

Motherhood as an Unexpected Business Accelerator

One of the most surprising insights from Gabrielle's journey is how motherhood actually improved her business performance. Conventional wisdom suggests that having a child will slow down or derail entrepreneurial momentum. Gabrielle's experience proves the opposite. Her best revenue year came when she was operating at just 30% capacity due to new motherhood.

The forced efficiency that comes with motherhood eliminated all the busy work that wasn't actually moving the needle. When you only have limited time and energy, you become laser-focused on what truly matters. The clarity that emerged from this constraint didn't just maintain her business—it accelerated it in ways that surprised everyone, including Gabrielle herself.

This revelation challenges the narrative that women must choose between serious ambition and motherhood. Instead, it suggests that the skills developed through motherhood—prioritization, emotional intelligence, efficiency, and the ability to see the bigger picture—are exactly the skills that create exceptional leaders.

Leading with Emotional Intelligence

Perhaps the most powerful aspect of Gabrielle's approach is her rejection of traditional hustle culture in favor of emotional intelligence. In a business world that often rewards those who can work the longest hours or push the hardest, she's proven that understanding emotions—both your own and others'—creates more sustainable and ultimately more profitable businesses.

This emotional approach extends to how she builds her team and company culture. Rather than extracting maximum productivity from her employees, she focuses on nurturing them to their next level of growth. This philosophy doesn't just create better work environments—it creates better business results. When people feel genuinely supported and understood, they bring their best work to everything they do.

Expanding Possibilities for Everyone

What emerges from Gabrielle's story is a broader truth about female leadership. Women leaders, when they embrace their authentic selves rather than trying to fit into traditional masculine leadership models, naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them. They create space for vulnerability, emotional intelligence, and different ways of thinking about success.

This expansion isn't just nice to have—it's a competitive advantage. In a world where authentic connection and genuine relationships matter more than ever, leaders who can create genuine human connections will consistently outperform those who rely solely on traditional business tactics.

Your Story Has Power

If you're sitting on a dream, carrying a story you think disqualifies you from success, or wondering if your struggles make you less capable of building something meaningful, Gabrielle's journey offers a different narrative. Your pain has purpose. Your story has power. The very things you think make you different or difficult might actually be your greatest business assets.

The key is learning to see your challenges through the lens of possibility rather than limitation. What looks like a barrier from one angle might be your unique competitive advantage from another. Gabrielle's success isn't despite her bipolar diagnosis—it's because she learned to channel the gifts that come with her particular brain chemistry into building something extraordinary.

This episode isn't just about one woman's remarkable journey from mental health struggles to business success. It's about reimagining what's possible when we stop trying to fit into other people's definitions of success and start building from our authentic selves. It's about recognizing that emotional intelligence, vulnerability, and different ways of thinking aren't weaknesses to overcome—they're strengths to leverage.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Get your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Superpower Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how your unique traits and experiences shape your leadership approach. Just like Gabrielle turned her bipolar diagnosis into a PR empire, your differences might be your greatest strengths.

questions.question: When facing a major setback or challenge, what's your natural response?

options.text: I dive deep into understanding what this experience is teaching me and how I can use it
I immediately start looking for new approaches or innovative solutions others haven't tried
I reach out to my network and have honest conversations about what I'm going through
I connect it back to my bigger why and figure out how this fits into my larger mission

questions.question: What energizes you most about leadership?

options.text: Helping people transform their struggles into strengths
Being first to market with game-changing ideas
Creating environments where people feel safe to be themselves
Building something that makes a lasting difference in the world

questions.question: How do you handle being different from other leaders in your industry?

options.text: I've learned that my differences are actually my competitive advantages
I use my unique perspective to spot opportunities others miss
I share my authentic story to help others feel less alone
I stay focused on my mission rather than trying to fit in

questions.question: When building your team, what matters most to you?

options.text: Finding people who understand that vulnerability creates strength
Attracting innovators who aren't afraid to challenge the status quo
Building genuine relationships based on trust and mutual respect
Aligning everyone around a shared sense of purpose and impact

questions.question: What's your relationship with traditional business advice?

options.text: I've learned to trust my emotional intelligence over generic formulas
I study it but often find myself creating entirely new approaches
I prefer learning from real conversations with other leaders
I filter everything through whether it aligns with my values and mission

questions.question: How do you view your past struggles or challenges?

options.text: They've given me depth and empathy that fuel my success today
They've taught me to think outside the box and find unconventional solutions
They've made me more authentic and able to connect with others
They've clarified my purpose and what I'm meant to contribute

results.title: The Emotional Alchemist
results.description: You have an incredible ability to transform difficult experiences into fuel for success. Your deep emotional intelligence allows you to connect authentically with others and see opportunities where others see obstacles. You don't just lead with your head—you lead with your whole heart, and that's what makes you unforgettable. This episode will show you how to harness your sensitivity as a competitive advantage in business.

results.title: The Strategic Pioneer
results.description: You're naturally drawn to innovation and have a gift for spotting trends before they become mainstream. You think differently and aren't afraid to take calculated risks that others might shy away from. Your leadership style combines vision with practical execution, making you someone others look to for direction. Gabrielle's story of being early to podcast PR will resonate deeply with your pioneering spirit.

results.title: The Authentic Connector
results.description: Your superpower lies in creating genuine relationships and building trust through vulnerability. You understand that real leadership isn't about having all the answers—it's about creating space for others to shine. You naturally expand possibilities for everyone around you through your inclusive approach. This episode will inspire you to lean even more into authenticity as your leadership edge.

results.title: The Purposeful Builder
results.description: You excel at turning your personal mission into meaningful work that impacts others. You understand that your story has power and you're not afraid to use it to create change. Your leadership style focuses on sustainable growth and helping others discover their own potential. Gabrielle's journey from diagnosis to million-dollar business will show you new ways to channel your purpose into profit.

transcript: [music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickus author of the manager entrepreneur and mama of six. Today's guest is someone who embodies the art of turning pain into purpose and purpose into impact. Gabrielle Scout Mayo is the founder and CEO of Scouts Agency and the go-to firm for entrepreneurs, authors, and thought leaders like me who want to amplify their message through the power of podcasts. But Scout's story didn't just start with success. It started with struggle. Before entrepreneurship, she was floundering, battling bipolar disorder, and searching for something that felt aligned. Then in 2018, she discovered entrepreneurship and everything changed. Within three months of starting her business in 2019, she quit her job and never looked back. Since then, Scout's agency has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes, pioneered the concept of the podcast tour, and helped hundreds of voices become movements. She's also the author of The Emotional Entrepreneur, a collection of 25 emotional lessons for anyone building a business from the inside out. Today we're talking about what happens when motherhood meets ambition, why being early in innovation is hard but worth it, and how emotional intelligence, not hustle, is the real secret to success. Welcome to the show, Scout. It's so nice to have you here today. >> Oh my gosh, I'm so honored. I've been looking forward to this. I'm really excited. Thank you. >> It's so fun. It's fun to have another mom, entrepreneur. You know, you've got littles to the little girl like toddler age like me. >> Singular singular, not plural. just one. Which is the hardest one? So if they can just hold each other's hands, let's go. >> Yeah, that's what I hear. That's what I hear. But one's been amazing. It's been definitely an adjustment. A lot of things that I know we'll get into, especially when it comes to business, that like you just you just don't know until you until you have a kid and you're living it. >> Oh, yeah. No, it all seems like so easy when you're watching it from the outside and then you're in it and you're like, "Wow, this is like 24/7 and it doesn't stop." >> I know. I remember being such an to my friends who didn't have kids. I'd be like, "Why can't you just get a babysitter and come to dinner with me?" You know, and looking back, I'm like, "Wow." Wow. I There's so many things that you once you have a kid that you realize are just very uh perspective altering for sure. Oh, yeah. It's big time. It's a big time shift. The world isn't about you anymore. Now, it's about this new little person. And it's kind of a It's a huge perspective shift. And as a woman in business, you know, it changes a lot of things. But before we get into all of that, before you became the founder of one of the leading podcast agencies, you said you were floundering and navigating bipolar disorder. >> Could you take us back to that chapter? What did finding entrepreneurship in 2018 unlock for you? I usually start my story at the age of 14 when I had my first depressive episode. And she said, "Sure." And the plan was we were going to print it at Kinko's and we were going to take all the photos with Polaroids and we were just going to pass it out to our friends. I dropped out of college. I just really wanted to make a name for myself and I wanted to start a business that would actually make me money and support me. I wanted to prove to the world that I could do this. And so I started So there was a little bit of that and that goes a long way. That's a big motivator. But when I started my podcast in in 2018 with my sister, which we we no longer do. We did it for 7 years. It was um run on one of the top networks. And that's something people don't realize about podcasting is how accessible it allows you to build a network. And what I saw happening was that when I would publish these episodes, I would watch our community follow our guests online, buy their product, sign up for their course, buy their book. And that's when I realized that being a guest on a podcast was a form of PR. And not just any form of PR, but one that had such deep ROI because it was so human. >> There's nowhere else in the social digital media landscape that you could go to and spend up to an hour with someone where they are speaking in a very intimate and vulnerable way. The way I'm talking with you now would be different if I was on stage talking to 2,000 people, right? Absolutely. And so people get to be a fly on the wall to very small intimate conversations and that helps them emotionally connect with the person and if they resonate and hear something in them that is going on in their life, they then are more it's like a stickier first impression and they're more prone to go follow them, support them, sign up for their business, etc. And so I saw it happening in real time and you know when I when I say the story and I'm I'm telling the origin story because it's going to get to why it became successful so quickly. When I say it back it sounds very polished. This was not as polished. You know I was like I'm going to try this. I saw something happening. I saw it working. I realized no one else was doing it and I was like let me try. And I think that is one of the biggest misconceptions about entrepreneurship and business is that you have this super clear idea in the beginning and the first thing you start is the home run. This was like my fifth thing that I started and it was just this willingness to try again and to be curious and to keep my eyes open for things that I felt I could fill in a market that I liked. And so I found a market I liked. I found something that wasn't happening and I was like let's try. I had a media kit. I emailed a thousand women the first 24 hours. Gmail blocked my email. It was a hunger and it was I found something that didn't exist. >> And people ask like, "Oh, well, did you know that sounds like you got lucky finding something you didn't exist?"
